title: Informative Maps
text:
Creating Choropleth Maps
In this page, I will be creating static and interactive choropleth maps using both numeric variable and the categorical variable. I will be using the the states dataset in the poliscidata package contains 135 variables on each of the 50 US states. The static plots will be created using the maps package and ggplot(), and the interactive plot will be created using the sf package and leaflet().


Data Frame

Static Map Dataset: U.S. State-Level Data on Car Fatalities and Political Leaning (2007)








Interactive Map Dataset: U.S. State Boundaries, Car Fatalities, and Political Leaning









I. Numeric Variable: Choropleth Maps Show Motor Vehicle Fatality Rates Vary Across U.S. States in 2007
I will create two choropleth maps to illustrate how motor vehicle fatality rates vary across U.S. states in 2007. The first choropleth map will be a static map, with color representing the number of fatalities per 100,000 people. Lighter colors (yellow and orange) indicate states with higher fatality rates, while darker colors (purple and blue) represent states with lower rates. The second will be an interactive map, where color indicates the number of fatalities per 100,000 people. States with darker shades of blue indicate higher fatality rates, while lighter shades represent lower rates. Although both maps display the same underlying data, they present the information in different ways, with the interactive map allowing users to explore the data more dynamically.

The Static Map











The Interactive Map









Conlcusion
By looking at these two maps, we can conclude that higher motor vehicle fatality rates are more common in southern states such as Mississippi and Alabama, while lower rates appear in northeastern states like New York and Massachusetts. The static map helps us quickly see these overall patterns, while the interactive map allows us to explore specific states and view exact values.


II. Categorical Variable: Political Leaning of U.S. States in 2007 (Cook Index)
Next, I will be creating another two choropleth maps displaying the political leaning of each U.S. state using categorical variable. The color represents the political leaning of each U.S. state based on the Cook Index categories. Each state is classified as “More Democratic,” “Even,” or “More Republican,” with different shades indicating each category. The first map will be the static map, where darker shades represent states that lean more Democratic, lighter shades represent states that lean more Republican, and intermediate shades indicate politically balanced states. The second map will be the interactive choropleth map, where blue indicating Democratic-leaning states, green representing politically balanced states, and tan indicating Republican-leaning states.

Static Map











The Interactive Map









Conclusion
These two maps show that democratic-leaning states are clearly concentrated along the West Coast and Northeast, including states such as California, Oregon, Washington, New York, and Massachusetts. In contrast, Republican-leaning states are more common in the South and parts of the central United States, including Texas, Oklahoma, Alabama, and Wyoming. States labeled as “Even,” such as Florida, Ohio, and Colorado, are scattered across different regions and may represent politically competitive or swing states.


Reference

states: https://www.rdocumentation.org/packages/maps/versions/3.4.2/topics/state
states_sf: https://rstudio.github.io/leaflet/json/us-states.geojson
poliscidata::states: https://rdrr.io/cran/poliscidata/man/states.html

title: Text Analysis
text:
Introduction
This project explores text analysis and sentiment analysis using a dataset of academic literature on racial and ethnic disparities in reproductive medicine in the United States. The primary goal is to develop practical skills in text mining, including the use of regular expressions, tokenization, and data visualization techniques.
The analysis begins by transforming the text into individual words (tokenization), followed by an exploration of the most frequently occurring terms. Common stopwords are then removed to focus on meaningful content. Finally, sentiment analysis is applied to classify the text as positive, negative, or neutral, providing insight into the overall tone and themes present in the literature.


Data Frame








Data Exploration

Stopwords Exploration

Snowball








Let’s remove the stopwords above from the Word variable (title) and create a table.

#REMOVE STOPWORDS & COUNT WORD FREQUENCY
new_data3 <- new_data1 |>
  anti_join(smart_stopwords) |>
  count(word, sort = TRUE) |> 
  filter(word != "NA") |>
  slice_max(n, n = 30)




10 Most Common Words In Title After Removing Stopwords

Word Frequency Bar Chart









This horizontal bar chart displays the frequency of the most common words in the dataset, where longer bars indicate higher word frequency. Words such as “cancer,” “disparities,” and “racial” appear most frequently, suggesting a strong focus on healthcare, particularly in oncology and health disparities. Because the chart is flipped, the word labels are shown on the y-axis, while the frequency values are displayed along the x-axis. This layout makes it easier to read longer words and compare their frequencies visually. Overall, the distribution highlights a clear thematic emphasis on cancer research, patient outcomes, and demographic disparities, providing insight into the primary topics present in the literature.

Implement Sentiment Analysis with the BING

Are titles more positve, negative, or neutral?


Data Table

First: Let’s visualize with bar chart









This faceted horizontal bar chart illustrates word frequencies categorized by sentiment in academic literature. Words associated with negative sentiment, including, cancer, risk, severe, appear in the left panel, while words associated with positive sentiment, survival, appear in the right panel. Bar lengths indicate relative word frequencies. The word that contributes the most to the sentiment scores for these titles is survival. All three terms—risk, cancer, and survival—show a maximum relative frequency of \(1.00\) within their respective sentiment categories.


Second: Let’s Visualize Using Wordcloud.









This word cloud visualizes the most frequently occurring terms in the literature dataset. The size of each word represents its frequency, making the most dominant themes immediately apparent. The largest words—including analysis, birth, black, cancer, and, risk, indicate that these are the most prominent terms in the text. Words are arranged in both horizontal and vertical orientations to create a compact thematic summary.



N-gram Analysis with Abstracts

First: I will begin by breaking the abstracts into bigrams, and the table belo is just showing only 10 example bigrams.

Fourth: Creating a Network Graph From the Bigram









This is a directed network graph illustrating common word pairs (bigrams) within the dataset. Each green node represents a unique word, while the arrows indicate the sequence and directional relationship of phrases appearing together in the text. The density of the arrows reveals thematic clusters, most notably a central focus on racial and ethnic disparities and a secondary cluster focused on maternal health and obstetric outcomes. Larger connections represent more frequent word pairings.
